questions.text: Which of the following is NOT one of the core activities of patient inspection?
Which of the following is an example of nonverbal communication?

answers.text: Asking the patient about their symptoms

questions.text: What is the distance range for social space?
Personal space is primarily used to:

answers.text: Garner sensitive patient information and allow the patient to be more comfortable sharing

questions.text: What is the distance range for intimate space?
Intimate space should only be entered:

answers.text: After establishing rapport and asking for permission
Only with a chaperone present at all times

questions.text: In the OLDCART framework, what does the 'A' stand for?
In OLDCART, which letter corresponds to 'What makes the symptom better?'
The cough reflex is stimulated by receptors located in the:
Cough is described as which of the following in patients with pulmonary disease?

answers.text: A rare symptom seen only in end-stage disease

questions.text: What is the difference between sputum and phlegm?

answers.text: Phlegm is expelled through coughing; sputum is not
Phlegm comes from the lungs and tracheobronchial tree and is NOT contaminated by oral secretions; sputum is expelled via coughing or throat clearing and may include oral secretions
Sputum is always infectious; phlegm is never infectious

questions.text: White-gray, thick, mucoid sputum is associated with all of the following EXCEPT:
Purulent (green, rusty, or yellow) sputum typically indicates:

answers.text: Infection — pneumonia caused by H. influenzae, streptococci, or staphylococci

questions.text: Frothy pink sputum is most commonly associated with:
Blood-streaked sputum (hemoptysis) can indicate:
Mucopurulent sputum can indicate:
A patient reports extremely sticky sputum. This consistency is described as:
Fetid (foul-smelling) sputum can indicate all of the following EXCEPT:
Bronchiectasis is best described as:

answers.text: A single acute infection of the pleura
Repeated lung infections/inflammation that dilates the bronchi and bronchioles
A tumor of the large airways

questions.text: Dyspnea is best defined as:

answers.text: An objective measurement of respiratory rate
A subjective experience of breathing discomfort — difficulty breathing as perceived by the patient
A type of abnormal breath sound heard on auscultation
A diagnosis code for all lung diseases

questions.text: In the 'DEATH' ADL mnemonic, what does the 'T' stand for?
All of the following are common causes of dyspnea EXCEPT:
The Modified Borg scale uses what numeric range?
Which dyspnea scale uses a 0–4 range and is typically used in conjunction with questionnaires to quantify dyspnea during ADLs?
The Dyspnea-12 Survey is utilized for patients with:
Pleuritic chest pain is typically described as:

answers.text: Sharp, stabbing pain normally felt during inspiration
A squeezing pressure radiating to the left arm
Burning pain that worsens after meals

questions.text: Dull, constant chest ache most likely reflects which origin of pulmonary-induced pain?
Peripheral (dependent) edema in right-heart failure is first seen in:

answers.text: The lower areas of the body

questions.text: Bilateral peripheral edema most commonly suggests:

answers.text: Pulmonary hypertension, heart failure, or venous insufficiency

questions.text: Unilateral peripheral edema is normally caused by:

answers.text: Venous obstruction or deep vein thrombosis (DVT)

questions.text: Pedal edema is a manifestation of:
Pain should be compared to what reference point?

answers.text: The pain experienced by an average adult
The worst pain the patient has ever experienced in their life
The patient's pain during their last hospitalization

questions.text: The Wong-Baker Faces scale is especially important for which population?
On the Wong-Baker Faces scale, what does a score of 0 represent?
Which level of consciousness describes a patient who is difficult to arouse and responds slowly?
A patient who responds only to vigorous or painful stimuli is best described as:
The Glasgow Coma Scale evaluates which THREE categories?

answers.text: Eye opening, verbal response, motor response
Heart rate, respiratory rate, blood pressure
Pupillary response, gag reflex, cough reflex

questions.text: What point range is given to the eye-opening category of the GCS?
What is the maximum point value for GCS motor response?
A patient whose eyes are open and focused, who recognizes you and follows eye movements, receives what GCS eye-opening score?
A patient who opens the eyes only when given painful stimuli receives what GCS eye-opening score?
Which GCS verbal score applies to a patient who can talk and speak coherently but is not entirely oriented to person, place, time, and event?
A patient making unintelligible words or sounds would score what on the GCS verbal component?
Decorticate posturing is classified as what GCS motor response score?
Decerebrate posturing is classified as what GCS motor response score?
Which description best matches DECORTICATE posturing?

answers.text: Body is abnormally extended, with arms and legs extended and rigid
Body curls into a protective posture by flexing the arms into the chest
Patient pulls away from painful stimulus

questions.text: Which description best matches DECEREBRATE posturing?

answers.text: Body is abnormally extended; arms and legs may be extended and very rigid or difficult to move
